questions.question: What is abstraction in Java? Why is it used?
keyPoints: Abstraction is the process of hiding complex implementation details and showing only essential features of an object
It reduces complexity by focusing on what an object does rather than how it does it
It enhances code reusability, maintainability, and allows loose coupling between components

questions.question: Differentiate between abstract class and interface in Java.
keyPoints: Abstract class can have both abstract and concrete methods; interface (pre-Java 8) has only abstract methods
Abstract class supports single inheritance; interface supports multiple inheritance
Abstract class can have constructors; interface cannot have constructors
Interface variables are by default public, static, and final; abstract class variables can be of any access specifier

questions.question: Can abstract classes have constructors? Justify your answer with reasoning.
keyPoints: Yes, abstract classes can have constructors
Constructors are used to initialize subclass objects via super() call
Even though abstract class cannot be instantiated, subclass objects call the parent abstract class constructor

questions.question: What are the rules for creating abstract methods in Java?
keyPoints: Abstract methods must be declared with 'abstract' keyword
They cannot have body/implementation (no curly braces)
They must end with a semicolon
They cannot be private, static, or final
If a class has any abstract method, the class must be declared abstract

questions.question: Explain the concept of 100% abstraction using interfaces.
keyPoints: Interfaces provide 100% abstraction as all methods are abstract
From Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods with implementation
All method signatures are hidden from the user
Multiple interfaces can be implemented by a class

questions.question: What happens when a subclass fails to implement all abstract methods?
keyPoints: If not all abstract methods are implemented, the subclass must be declared abstract
If the subclass is not declared abstract, a compile-time error occurs
This allows progressive implementation of abstract methods in inheritance hierarchy

questions.question: Explain the relationship between abstraction and encapsulation with examples.
keyPoints: Abstraction focuses on hiding what objects do (behavior)
Encapsulation focuses on hiding how data is stored and manipulated
Encapsulation is the implementation mechanism that achieves abstraction
Example: ATM machine abstracts the transaction process; encapsulation bundles account balance with deposit/withdraw methods

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating the use of abstract class and interface. Create an abstract class 'Shape' with an abstract method 'area()' and an interface 'Drawable' with a method 'draw()'. Implement these in concrete classes.
keyPoints: Define abstract class Shape with abstract method area() returning double
Create interface Drawable with abstract method draw()
Implement concrete class Circle extending Shape, implementing Drawable
Implement concrete class Rectangle extending Shape, implementing Drawable
Demonstrate polymorphism by creating array of Shape references
Call area() and draw() methods on objects

questions.question: Explain the differences between abstract classes and interfaces in detail, considering pre-Java 8 and post-Java 8 scenarios.
keyPoints: Pre-Java 8: Interface had only abstract methods; abstract class had both abstract and concrete methods
Pre-Java 8: Interface variables were implicitly public, static, final; abstract class had regular instance variables
Pre-Java 8: Interface supported multiple inheritance; abstract class supported single inheritance
Post-Java 8: Interfaces can have default and static methods with implementation
Post-Java 8: Interfaces can have private methods (from Java 9)
Use abstract class when there is IS-A relationship and common code; use interface for HAS-A capability

questions.question: Design a real-world scenario demonstrating abstraction in Java. Create an abstract class 'BankAccount' with abstract methods 'deposit()' and 'withdraw()', and an interface 'Transaction' with method 'processTransaction()'. Show how abstraction helps in managing complexity.
keyPoints: Create abstract class BankAccount with accountNumber, balance fields and abstract methods
Create interface Transaction with processTransaction() method
Implement SavingsAccount and CurrentAccount classes
Show how bank customers only see deposit/withdraw methods, not implementation details
Demonstrate how changes in implementation (e.g., database storage) don't affect client code
Explain how abstraction reduces complexity and provides maintainability

questions.question: What are the restrictions and rules for abstract classes in Java? Explain with examples why each rule exists.
keyPoints: Cannot instantiate abstract class directly - prevents creation of incomplete objects
Can have abstract methods (without body) - defines contract for subclasses
Can have concrete methods with full implementation - provides reusable code
Can have constructors - for subclass initialization via super()
Can have static methods - utility methods accessible without instantiation
Cannot be final - must be extensible
Cannot combine with final modifier - contradictory requirements
May or may not have abstract methods - provides flexibility
